walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Labyrinth
walkthrough.text: Welcome to Onirim. You are a Dreamwalker, lost in a mysterious labyrinth of dreams. Your quest is to find the eight oneiric Doors before your dream fades away. This is a solo or cooperative two-player card game where you play against the deck itself. Let's set everything up so you can begin your journey through the dream world.

walkthrough.title: Identify the Card Types
walkthrough.text: Before we shuffle anything, let's get familiar with the cards. The base game has 76 cards total, divided into three types. First, there are 58 Location cards in four colors: red (Observatory, 16 cards), blue (Aquarium, 15 cards), green (Garden, 14 cards), and brown (Library, 13 cards). Each Location card has one of three symbols: a Sun, a Moon, or a Key. Second, there are 8 Door cards, two in each color. Third, there are 10 Nightmare cards, which are the Dream cards of the base game. Set aside any expansion cards if you have them. Look for the expansion symbol in the lower right corner and remove those.

walkthrough.title: Understand the Location Cards
walkthrough.text: Take a closer look at the Location cards. Each one shows a color and a symbol. Suns are the most common, Moons are less common, and Keys are the rarest and most powerful. The Key cards are special because discarding one triggers a Prophecy, and holding one lets you claim a Door when you draw it. You will be playing and discarding these cards throughout the game, so get familiar with what they look like in each color.

walkthrough.title: Know Your Door Cards
walkthrough.text: The 8 Door cards are the object of your quest. There are two Doors for each of the four colors: two red Observatory Doors, two blue Aquarium Doors, two green Garden Doors, and two brown Library Doors. Having all 8 Door cards on the table at the same time is how you win the game. These cards start shuffled into the deck and you will discover them as you explore the labyrinth.

walkthrough.title: Meet the Nightmares
walkthrough.text: The 10 Nightmare cards are the lurking horrors of the labyrinth. These dark Dream cards are shuffled into the deck alongside everything else. Whenever you draw one, you must immediately resolve a penalty. There is no avoiding them, only choosing which price to pay. We will cover the four penalty options in the rules section. For now, just know they are in the deck, waiting.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Deck
walkthrough.text: Now take all 76 base game cards, including the Location cards, Door cards, and Nightmare cards, and shuffle them thoroughly into one face-down deck. This deck represents the labyrinth you are about to explore. Place it in the center of the table where you can easily reach it. Make sure the shuffle is thorough since the distribution of Nightmares and Doors matters a great deal.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Play Areas
walkthrough.text: Clear space for four areas around the deck. First, a spot for the discard pile, which starts empty. Second, a space in front of you for your Labyrinth row, where you will play Location cards in a line. Third, an area for your acquired Door cards, where you will proudly display each Door you find. Fourth, a temporary area called the Limbo pile, where cards go before being shuffled back into the deck. The Limbo pile is important and we will use it during setup.

walkthrough.title: Draw Your Opening Hand
walkthrough.text: Now draw 5 cards from the top of the deck to form your opening hand. Here is the crucial rule: your starting hand must contain only Location cards. If you draw any Door cards or Nightmare cards, set them aside into the Limbo pile and draw replacement cards from the deck. Keep doing this until you have exactly 5 Location cards in your hand. You might need to set aside several cards before you get a clean hand of 5 Locations.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Limbo Pile Back In
walkthrough.text: Once you have your 5 Location cards in hand, take all the cards you set aside in the Limbo pile and shuffle them back into the deck. This is important because those Door cards and Nightmares need to be waiting for you somewhere in the labyrinth. After shuffling, the Limbo pile should be empty and you are ready to begin.

walkthrough.title: Two-Player Setup Differences
walkthrough.text: If you are playing with two players, the setup changes slightly. Shuffle the 76-card deck and reveal the top 8 cards. Set aside any Door or Dream cards into the Limbo pile and replace them until you have 8 Location cards showing. Shuffle the Limbo pile back into the deck. Now each player takes turns picking one card at a time from the 8 revealed cards until each player has 3 cards. These are your Personal resources. The 2 remaining cards become the Shared resources and stay face up on the table. Your effective hand is your 3 Personal cards plus the 2 Shared cards.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Dream
walkthrough.text: Your setup is complete. You should have a shuffled deck in the center, an empty discard pile, an empty Labyrinth row, space for your acquired Doors, and 5 Location cards in your hand (or 3 Personal plus 2 Shared in a two-player game). The dream labyrinth stretches before you, full of hidden Doors and lurking Nightmares. Take a breath and step into the dream. Your quest begins now.

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure Overview
walkthrough.text: Each turn in Onirim follows three phases in order. Phase 1: Play or discard a card from your hand. Phase 2: Fill your hand back to 5 cards by drawing from the deck. Phase 3: Shuffle any cards in the Limbo pile back into the deck. You repeat this cycle, turn after turn, until you either find all 8 Doors and win, or the deck runs out and you lose. The rhythm of the game flows from these three simple phases, but the decisions within them are where the real challenge lives.

walkthrough.title: Phase 1 Option A: Play a Location Card
walkthrough.text: Your first option in Phase 1 is to play a Location card from your hand face up into your Labyrinth row. This row builds up in front of you, card by card, representing the path you are exploring through the dream. There is one golden rule you must always obey: the card you play cannot share a symbol with the last card in your Labyrinth row. The three symbols are Sun, Moon, and Key. So if the last card you played has a Sun, your next card must show a Moon or a Key. Color does not matter for this rule, only the symbol.

walkthrough.title: Discovering a Door
walkthrough.text: Here is where the magic happens. If the card you just played is the third consecutive card of the same color in your Labyrinth row, you immediately discover a Door. Search through the deck for one Door card of that color, place it face up in your acquired Doors area, then shuffle the deck. Important: if a fourth card of the same color follows, it counts as the first card of a new series, not the fourth of the old one. The series resets at three. Also, the three cards must be truly consecutive. If a different color breaks the streak, the series is broken.

walkthrough.title: Phase 1 Option B: Discard a Card
walkthrough.text: Your second option in Phase 1 is to discard a card from your hand to the discard pile instead of playing it. If you discard a Sun or Moon card, nothing special happens and you move to Phase 2. But if the discarded card is a Key, something powerful is triggered: a Prophecy. Look at the top 5 cards of the deck. You must discard one of them, then arrange the remaining 4 cards back on top of the deck in any order you choose. If fewer than 5 cards remain, reveal them all, discard one, and reorder the rest. Prophecy gives you precious information and control over what is coming next.

walkthrough.title: Phase 2: Fill Your Hand
walkthrough.text: After playing or discarding, you must draw cards from the deck until you have 5 cards in hand again. Usually you only need to draw one card, since you played or discarded one. When you draw, there are three possibilities. If it is a Location card, it simply goes into your hand. If it is a Door card, you have a choice. If it is a Dream card (a Nightmare in the base game), you must resolve it immediately. Let's cover the Door and Nightmare cases in detail.

walkthrough.title: Drawing a Door Card
walkthrough.text: When you draw a Door card during Phase 2, you may immediately discard a Key card of the same color from your hand. If you do, you claim that Door and place it in your acquired Doors area. This is the second way to obtain Doors, and it is why Key cards are so valuable. Holding a Key of the right color at the right moment can save you from needing to build a three-card sequence. If you do not have a matching Key or choose not to use one, the Door card goes into the Limbo pile and will be shuffled back into the deck later.

walkthrough.title: Resolving a Nightmare
walkthrough.text: When you draw a Nightmare card, dread fills the labyrinth. You must choose one of four penalties. Option I: Discard a Key card from your hand. Option II: Place one of your already-acquired Door cards into the Limbo pile, losing it back into the deck. Option III: Reveal the top 5 cards of the deck and discard all of them except any Door or Dream cards, which go to the Limbo pile instead. Option IV: Discard your entire hand and draw a new hand of 5 Location cards using the same method as during setup (setting aside Doors and Dreams to the Limbo pile). After resolving the Nightmare, discard the Nightmare card itself.

walkthrough.title: Choosing Your Nightmare Penalty
walkthrough.text: Each Nightmare penalty has different weight depending on your situation. Discarding a Key is cheapest if you have a spare Key you do not need urgently. Losing a Door is devastating but sometimes necessary to protect your hand. Revealing the top 5 cards thins the deck, which can be dangerous if the deck is already running low, but at least you keep your hand and Doors intact. Discarding your whole hand is the nuclear option, wiping your plans clean but guaranteeing you keep all your Doors. There is no right answer. Read the state of the game and choose the least harmful option.

walkthrough.title: Phase 3: Shuffle the Limbo Pile
walkthrough.text: At the end of each turn, if any cards accumulated in the Limbo pile during this turn, shuffle them all back into the deck. This can happen from unclaimed Door cards, from Nightmare resolution effects, or from other game events. If no cards went to the Limbo pile this turn, skip this phase entirely. The turn is now over. Begin your next turn with Phase 1.

walkthrough.title: Victory and Defeat
walkthrough.text: You win the game the instant all 8 Door cards are on the table in front of you. The game ends immediately. You do not need to finish your turn or fill your hand. You have escaped the labyrinth of dreams. You lose if you ever need to draw a card to fill your hand and there are no cards left in the deck. The dream has ended and you are trapped forever. Every card you play, discard, or lose to a Nightmare brings you closer to one of these two fates.

walkthrough.title: Two-Player Rule Differences
walkthrough.text: In the two-player game, each player builds their own separate Labyrinth row. You cannot play cards into the other player's row. When discarding a card, the active player may also swap a card from their Personal resources with a card from the Shared resources. Players take alternating turns. Victory requires each player to have 4 Door cards, one of each color. When one player completes their set, they keep playing until both players have all 4. You can agree before the game whether to play open (share information freely) or closed (no discussion of Personal resources).

summary.title: Turn Structure Overview
bullets: Phase 1: Play or discard a card
Phase 2: Fill your hand to 5 cards
Phase 3: Shuffle the Limbo pile back into the deck

A 4th same-color card resets as the 1st of a new series
A different color between same-color cards breaks the streak

Sun or Moon: nothing special — proceed to Phase 2

Door card: claim it with a matching Key, or it goes to Limbo

walkthrough.title: Entering the Dream
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through the first several turns of a solo game with a scripted hand. Follow along with real cards if you have them, or just read through to see how the game flows. You are the Dreamwalker, alone in the labyrinth. Your quest: find all 8 Doors before the deck runs out. Let's begin.

walkthrough.title: Your Starting Hand
walkthrough.text: After setup, your hand contains these 5 Location cards: a red Sun, a red Moon, a blue Sun, a green Key, and a brown Moon. The Labyrinth row in front of you is empty. The discard pile is empty. You have no Doors yet. Let's plan our first few moves.

walkthrough.title: Turn 1: Play the Red Sun
walkthrough.text: For your first play, place the red Sun card as the first card in your Labyrinth row. Since the row was empty, there is no symbol restriction. This is the beginning of your path through the dream. Now move to Phase 2 and draw a card. You draw a red Key. Great, you now have: red Moon, blue Sun, green Key, brown Moon, red Key.

walkthrough.title: Turn 2: Play the Red Moon
walkthrough.text: You want to build a sequence of 3 red cards to discover a red Door. The last card in your Labyrinth row is a red Sun. Can you play the red Moon next? Check the golden rule: the red Moon has a Moon symbol and the last card has a Sun symbol. Different symbols, so yes, it is legal. Play the red Moon. Your Labyrinth row is now: red Sun, red Moon. Two reds in a row. One more and you discover a Door. Draw a card. You get a blue Moon.

walkthrough.title: Turn 3: Play the Red Key for the Door
walkthrough.text: The last card in your row is a red Moon. Your red Key has a Key symbol, which is different from Moon, so it is legal to play. Place the red Key as the third consecutive red card. Three reds in a row! You immediately search the deck for a red Observatory Door card and place it in your acquired Doors area. Shuffle the deck afterward. You have your first Door. Draw a card to refill. You get a green Sun.

walkthrough.title: What Just Happened
walkthrough.text: Let's pause and appreciate the mechanics at work. You played three consecutive Location cards of the same color (red), each with a different symbol from the card before it. Sun, then Moon, then Key. That sequence discovered a Door. This is the primary way you will find Doors in the game. Notice that you used your precious red Key to complete the sequence. Key cards are rare, so spending one in a sequence is a real cost. Sometimes it is better to hold the Key for claiming a drawn Door instead.

walkthrough.title: Turn 4: Starting a New Color
walkthrough.text: Your hand is now: blue Sun, green Key, brown Moon, blue Moon, green Sun. Your Labyrinth row ends with a red Key. Let's start building toward a blue Door. Play the blue Sun. The last card was a Key (red), and you are playing a Sun (blue). Different symbols, legal move. Your Labyrinth row now has the blue Sun at the end. Draw a card. You get a brown Sun.

walkthrough.title: Turn 5: Continuing Blue
walkthrough.text: The last card is a blue Sun. You have a blue Moon in hand. Moon is different from Sun, so it is legal. Play the blue Moon. Two blue cards in a row. Draw a card. Oh no, it is a Nightmare card. You must resolve it immediately before continuing to fill your hand.

walkthrough.title: Facing Your First Nightmare
walkthrough.text: Time to choose a penalty. Let's evaluate your options. Option I: Discard a Key from hand. You have the green Key. Losing it hurts your future green Door prospects. Option II: Place an acquired Door in Limbo. You only have one red Door, and losing it would undo your progress. Option III: Reveal top 5 deck cards, discard all Locations, Limbo any Doors or Dreams. This thins the deck but you keep your hand and Door. Option IV: Discard entire hand and redraw. You would lose the green Key and your planned blue cards.

walkthrough.title: Choosing the Penalty
walkthrough.text: In this case, let's choose Option I and discard the green Key. Why? Your red Door is too precious to lose to Limbo. Revealing 5 cards could thin the deck dangerously. And discarding your whole hand would destroy your blue sequence plans. The green Key is valuable, but it is the smallest sacrifice right now. Discard the green Key, then discard the Nightmare card itself. Continue drawing to fill your hand. You draw a red Moon. Your hand is now: brown Moon, green Sun, brown Sun, red Moon.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Forgetting the Symbol Rule
walkthrough.text: Let's say you are excited about continuing your blue sequence and you draw a blue Sun next turn. Your Labyrinth row ends with a blue Moon. Can you play the blue Sun? Check the symbols: Moon and Sun. Different symbols, so yes! But imagine you drew a blue Moon instead. Could you play it? No! The last card is already a blue Moon. Same symbol (Moon), so it is forbidden. Many new players forget this rule when they are focused on matching colors. Always check the symbol, not just the color.

walkthrough.title: Turn 6: A Discarding Decision
walkthrough.text: Your hand is brown Moon, green Sun, brown Sun, red Moon, and let's say you draw a green Moon. The Labyrinth row ends with a blue Moon. You could play the green Sun (different symbol from Moon: Sun) to start a green sequence, or the brown Sun. But maybe none of your cards advance a useful sequence right now. Sometimes the best move is to discard. If you discard the brown Sun (a Sun card), nothing special happens and you draw a replacement. If you discard a Key, you get a Prophecy. Discarding is not failure. It is strategy.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Wasting Keys on Sequences
walkthrough.text: Here is a mistake many new players make: they always play Key cards into the Labyrinth row to complete three-in-a-row sequences. But Keys have a second use that is equally powerful. If you draw a Door from the deck and you hold a Key of the same color, you can discard the Key to claim the Door instantly. Plus, if you discard a Key during Phase 1, you get a Prophecy, which lets you peek at and rearrange the top 5 cards. Think carefully before spending a Key in a Labyrinth sequence. Sometimes holding it is better.

walkthrough.title: Turn 7: Using a Prophecy
walkthrough.text: Let's say your hand has a brown Key and you decide to discard it for a Prophecy instead of playing it. Place the brown Key on the discard pile. Now look at the top 5 cards of the deck. Suppose you see: a brown Moon, a Nightmare, a blue Key, a green Door, and a red Sun. You must discard one of these 5 cards. The Nightmare is the most dangerous, so discard it. Now arrange the remaining 4 in any order on top of the deck. Put the green Door on top so you draw it next, followed by the blue Key, then the others. You have just shaped the future of your dream.

walkthrough.title: Turn 8: Claiming a Door with a Key
walkthrough.text: Following your Prophecy, you arranged a green Door on top. Draw it during Phase 2. You draw the green Door card. Now, do you have a green Key in hand? If yes, discard the green Key to claim the Door immediately. If no, the Door goes to the Limbo pile and gets shuffled back into the deck at the end of your turn. This is why holding Keys is so valuable. One Key in hand at the right moment can save you from building an entire three-card color sequence.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Ignoring Nightmare Count
walkthrough.text: There are 10 Nightmares in the deck. As you play, keep a rough mental count of how many you have encountered. If you have seen 6 or 7 Nightmares already, the remaining deck is much safer. If you have only seen 1 or 2, brace yourself. This awareness should influence your Nightmare penalty choices. Early on, protect your hand and accept losing a Key. Later, when Nightmares thin out, you can afford to hold Keys more aggressively for Door claims.

walkthrough.title: The Middle Game
walkthrough.text: By now, you should have 2 or 3 Doors acquired and roughly half the deck remaining. The middle game is where resource management becomes critical. Look at your hand: which colors do you still need Doors for? Do you have Key cards that match those colors? Is your Labyrinth row building toward a useful three-in-a-row? Each turn, weigh whether to play a card and build your row, or discard strategically to trigger Prophecies or set up better future draws.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Tension
walkthrough.text: As the deck shrinks, tension rises. If you still need 2 or 3 Doors, every draw feels heavy. Each Nightmare penalty becomes more painful because Option III (reveal top 5) devours what little deck you have left. In the endgame, prioritize holding Keys to claim drawn Doors rather than building Labyrinth sequences, which require playing 3 cards and thinning the deck even further. Speed matters now.

walkthrough.title: Victory or Defeat
walkthrough.text: The game ends one of two ways. If you acquire all 8 Door cards, you win instantly. The dream releases you. If the deck runs out while you still need to draw, you lose. The dream has consumed you. Most games of Onirim come down to the final 10-15 cards. That tense feeling of racing against the shrinking deck is the heart of the game.

walkthrough.title: Before You Dream Again
walkthrough.text: You have now walked through a full game arc: setup, early turns, building sequences, facing Nightmares, using Prophecies, claiming Doors with Keys, and the endgame race. Remember the three common mistakes: always check symbols before playing a card, think before using Key cards in sequences, and track the Nightmare count. Ready to explore on your own? Check out General Tips for deeper strategy and the Appendix for quick reference on all card types and terms. Ask your GM Guide anytime you have a question. Good luck, Dreamwalker.

Check General Tips for deeper strategy
Ask your GM Guide anytime during play

walkthrough.title: The Key Card Dilemma
walkthrough.text: Key cards are the rarest and most versatile cards in the game, and how you use them defines your strategy. Keys have three possible uses: play them in a Labyrinth sequence to complete a three-in-a-row, discard them during Phase 1 to trigger a Prophecy, or hold them to claim a Door drawn during Phase 2. New players tend to default to playing Keys in sequences, but experienced Dreamwalkers know that holding a Key for the right moment is often more powerful. A Key in hand is insurance against the unknown.

walkthrough.title: When to Trigger a Prophecy
walkthrough.text: Discarding a Key for a Prophecy gives you rare control over your fate. You see the top 5 cards, remove one, and arrange the rest. This is most powerful when you can remove a Nightmare and arrange a Door or Key on top. Prophecy is especially valuable in the mid-game when you need to plan sequences and want to avoid nasty surprises. In the late game, when the deck is thin, every card matters even more, making Prophecy a lifeline. If you have a Key in a color where you have already found both Doors, that Key's best use is almost always a Prophecy.

walkthrough.title: Color Priority Planning
walkthrough.text: At the start of the game, look at your hand and decide which color to pursue first. Focus on the color where you have the most cards or the best symbol variety. Building toward one color at a time is more efficient than trying to advance all four simultaneously. Remember the card distribution: red has 16 Location cards, blue has 15, green has 14, and brown has 13. Brown is hardest because it has the fewest Location cards and therefore fewer chances to draw what you need. If you have brown cards early, consider prioritizing them before they become scarce.

walkthrough.title: Managing Your Hand
walkthrough.text: Your hand of 5 cards is your entire world in Onirim. Try to maintain diversity: cards in at least two or three colors give you flexibility. If your hand fills up with cards of one color, you are fragile, because a Nightmare that forces a hand discard will wipe out all your progress in that color. Conversely, holding a Key card of a color you need is always worth preserving if you can. Before each turn, scan your hand and the Labyrinth row. Ask: what can I play legally? What sequence am I building? And what will I do if I draw a Nightmare?

walkthrough.title: The Discard Is Not a Waste
walkthrough.text: New players feel bad about discarding because it feels like losing a card. But discarding is a strategic tool. First, it removes a card you do not need, making room for a fresh draw that might be better. Second, if you discard a Key, you get a Prophecy, which is incredibly powerful. Third, sometimes your hand is clogged with cards that do not advance any sequence, and discarding clears the jam. Think of discarding as pruning a garden. You are making space for something better to grow.

walkthrough.title: Nightmare Preparation
walkthrough.text: With 10 Nightmares in a 76-card deck, you will encounter them roughly every 7-8 draws. Always have a plan for when one appears. The cheapest penalty is usually discarding a Key you do not critically need. If you have no Keys, you face harder choices. Losing a Door is painful but recoverable since the Door goes to Limbo and back into the deck. Revealing the top 5 is neutral early on but dangerous when the deck is thin. Discarding your hand is the reset button — painful, but you keep all your Doors. The best defense against Nightmares is keeping a Key in hand as a safety net.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Deck
walkthrough.text: As the game progresses, pay attention to what has been discarded and what Doors remain in the deck. If you have discarded two green Keys, you know that building green sequences is your only path to green Doors. If most of the red cards have been discarded or played, remaining red cards in the deck are rare. This information should guide your priorities. In the late game, when the deck is thin, you can sometimes calculate exactly what remains and make near-perfect decisions.

walkthrough.title: Embrace the Uncertainty
walkthrough.text: Onirim is a game of imperfect information, and some games are unwinnable due to shuffling luck. Do not be discouraged by losses. A good win rate for experienced players is around 50-60 percent. Each game teaches you something about probability, risk assessment, and resource management. The dream labyrinth is different every time you enter it. Enjoy the tension of each draw, celebrate clever Prophecy plays, and accept that sometimes the Nightmares win. That unpredictability is what makes each victory feel earned.

summary.title: The Key Card Dilemma
bullets: Keys have 3 uses: play in sequence, claim drawn Door, trigger Prophecy
Holding a Key is often more valuable than playing it immediately
A Key in hand is insurance against unexpected Door draws

summary.title: When to Trigger a Prophecy
bullets: Discard a Key to peek at top 5 cards, remove 1, reorder 4
Best use: remove a Nightmare, arrange a Door/Key on top
Especially valuable mid-to-late game when deck is shrinking
Keys for colors where both Doors are found should become Prophecies

summary.title: Color Priority Planning
bullets: Focus on one color at a time for efficiency
Card distribution: red 16, blue 15, green 14, brown 13
Brown is hardest (fewest cards) — pursue it early if possible
Start with the color you have the most cards or best symbol variety for

summary.title: Managing Your Hand
bullets: Maintain 2-3 colors for flexibility
Mono-color hand = fragile against Nightmare hand-discard penalty
Always know what you can play legally and what sequence you're building
Plan your Nightmare response before drawing

summary.title: The Discard Is Not a Waste
bullets: Discarding removes clutter and draws fresh cards
Key discard = Prophecy (powerful deck control)
Sometimes no card advances a sequence — discard the weakest
Think of it as pruning for better future draws

summary.title: Nightmare Preparation
bullets: Expect a Nightmare roughly every 7-8 draws (10 in 76 cards)
Cheapest penalty: discard a spare Key
Keep at least one Key in hand as a safety net
Know your backup plan before each draw

summary.title: Reading the Deck
bullets: Track discarded Keys and Locations by color
If all Keys of a color are gone, sequences are the only path for that color
Late game: calculate remaining cards for near-perfect decisions
Guide your color priorities based on what remains

summary.title: Embrace the Uncertainty
bullets: Good win rate for experienced players: 50-60%
Some games are unwinnable due to shuffle — don't be discouraged
Each game teaches probability and resource management
The tension of each draw is what makes victories satisfying

walkthrough.title: Key Card Economy
walkthrough.text: At the advanced level, Key management is the most important skill. There are a limited number of Key cards in each color: 3 in red (Observatory), 3 in blue (Aquarium), 2 in green (Garden), and 2 in brown (Library). Every Key you spend in a Labyrinth sequence is one less Key available for Door claims and Prophecies. The optimal approach is to hold Keys for as long as possible, using Sun and Moon cards for sequences when you can. Spend a Key in a sequence only when it is the only way to complete a critical three-in-a-row, and no alternative exists.

walkthrough.title: Prophecy Optimization
walkthrough.text: When you trigger a Prophecy, your discard and reorder choices have cascading effects. Priority one: discard a Nightmare if you see one. This permanently removes a threat from the deck, which is better than most other options. Priority two: if no Nightmare is present, discard a Location card in a color where you have already found both Doors. Priority three: arrange the remaining cards so that a Door is on top only if you have a matching Key in hand, or arrange three same-color cards in legal symbol order to set up a sequence on future turns. Think multiple turns ahead when reordering.

walkthrough.title: Deck Thinning Awareness
walkthrough.text: Every card that leaves the deck through play, discard, or Nightmare penalties brings you closer to either victory or defeat. The deck starts at 71 cards after your opening hand of 5. Each turn you play or discard 1 card and draw 1, so the deck shrinks by roughly 1 card per turn. Nightmare penalties can devour 5 cards at once (Option III). After each Nightmare, assess the deck thickness. If fewer than 20 cards remain and you still need 3 or more Doors, shift entirely to Key-based Door claiming rather than sequence building. Sequences consume 3 cards to gain 1 Door; Keys consume just 1.

walkthrough.title: When to Sacrifice a Door
walkthrough.text: Option II of Nightmare resolution — placing an acquired Door back into the Limbo pile — feels devastating, but sometimes it is the correct play. If you have 5 or 6 Doors and a hand full of Key cards that are about to complete your remaining Doors, losing one Door to the Limbo pile is recoverable. The Door goes back into the deck, and you may draw it again or find it through a Prophecy. In contrast, discarding your hand (Option IV) would lose those critical Keys. Evaluate what is harder to replace: the Door or the hand. In the late game with a thin deck, the Door is often easier to re-acquire.

walkthrough.title: Symbol Sequencing Strategy
walkthrough.text: The golden rule says consecutive cards in your Labyrinth row cannot share a symbol. With three symbols (Sun, Moon, Key), the legal patterns for a three-in-a-row are: Sun-Moon-Key, Sun-Key-Moon, Moon-Sun-Key, Moon-Key-Sun, Key-Sun-Moon, and Key-Moon-Sun. When planning a sequence, check which symbols you actually have in the target color. If you only have Sun and Moon cards in blue, you can still build a two-card run but will need a Key to finish. This is another reason to track what symbols remain in each color. Sometimes a sequence is simply impossible because the right symbols have been discarded.

walkthrough.title: Nightmare Penalty Meta-Strategy
walkthrough.text: Across a full game, you will face roughly 7-9 of the 10 Nightmares. Plan your penalty choices as a portfolio, not individually. Early game (Doors 0-2): prefer discarding Keys since you have more time to draw replacements. Mid game (Doors 3-5): switch to Option III (reveal 5) when your Key supply is low, using it to thin Nightmares from the deck. Late game (Doors 6-7): protect your hand and Doors at almost any cost. Sacrifice a Door (Option II) only if you can immediately re-acquire it. The shifting penalty priority reflects the changing value of resources as the game progresses.

walkthrough.title: Two-Player Advanced Coordination
walkthrough.text: In the two-player game, the Shared resources add a coordination layer. The two face-up Shared cards are visible to both players, so they serve as a communication channel even in a closed-information game. When you swap a Personal card into the Shared resources, you are signaling what you need or do not need. Advanced two-player pairs develop implicit signals: placing a Key in Shared resources might mean you do not need that color, and your partner should take it. Each player needs exactly one Door of each color, so specialize early — one player focuses on red and green sequences while the other pursues blue and brown, sharing Keys as needed.

walkthrough.title: Win Rate Optimization
walkthrough.text: Experienced Onirim players win roughly 50-65 percent of solo games. To push toward the higher end, internalize three habits. First, always count remaining Nightmares. If 7 have appeared, the remaining deck is much safer and you can play more aggressively. Second, track Keys by color. Know exactly how many Keys of each color remain in play, in the discard, and potentially in the deck. Third, use Prophecies to remove Nightmares rather than to arrange favorable draws. Removing a Nightmare permanently reduces the penalty pressure for the rest of the game, which compounds into a higher overall win rate.

summary.title: Key Card Economy
bullets: Key distribution: red 3, blue 3, green 2, brown 2
Hold Keys for Door claims and Prophecies whenever possible
Spend Key in a sequence only as a last resort
Sun and Moon cards should do the heavy lifting in Labyrinth rows

summary.title: Prophecy Optimization
bullets: Priority 1: discard a Nightmare (permanent removal)
Priority 2: discard a Location in a completed-Door color
Priority 3: arrange cards to set up future sequences or Door draws
Think multiple turns ahead when reordering

summary.title: Deck Thinning Awareness
bullets: Deck starts at ~71 cards after opening hand
Nightmare Option III can devour 5 cards at once
Below 20 cards with 3+ Doors needed: switch to Key-based claiming
Sequences cost 3 cards per Door; Keys cost 1

summary.title: When to Sacrifice a Door
bullets: Losing a Door to Limbo is recoverable — it re-enters the deck
Correct when hand contains critical Keys for remaining Doors
Evaluate: which is harder to replace, the Door or the hand?

summary.title: Symbol Sequencing Strategy
bullets: 6 legal three-symbol patterns: any permutation of Sun, Moon, Key
Check which symbols you have in the target color before committing
Track discarded symbols — a sequence may be impossible
Two symbols available still needs a Key to finish (trade-off)

summary.title: Nightmare Penalty Meta-Strategy
bullets: Early (0-2 Doors): discard Keys, time to draw replacements
Mid (3-5 Doors): use Option III to thin deck and remove Nightmares
Late (6-7 Doors): protect hand and Doors at almost any cost

summary.title: Two-Player Advanced Coordination
bullets: Shared resources = visible communication channel
Placing a Key in Shared signals you don't need that color
Specialize: one player focuses on 2 colors, partner on the other 2
Share Keys across the color divide for efficient Door claiming

summary.title: Win Rate Optimization
bullets: Target win rate: 50-65% with disciplined play
Count remaining Nightmares for risk assessment
Track Keys by color (in hand, discarded, remaining in deck)
Use Prophecies to remove Nightmares — compounds into higher win rate

entries.definition: The player character, lost in the labyrinth of dreams. In a solo game, you are the lone Dreamwalker. In a two-player game, both players are Dreamwalkers exploring different parts of the same labyrinth.
The 8 Door cards that are the object of your quest. Having all 8 on the table simultaneously is the victory condition. There are 2 Doors in each of the four colors.
The line of Location cards you build in front of you by playing cards during Phase 1. Each new card is placed overlapping the previous one. This row represents your path through the dream labyrinth.
A temporary holding area for cards that will be shuffled back into the deck at the end of the turn (Phase 3). Used during setup, when Doors are not claimed, and during Nightmare resolution.
The main playable cards in the game. 58 total across four colors. Each has a color and one of three symbols (Sun, Moon, Key). Played into the Labyrinth row or discarded from hand.

The 8 cards you are trying to collect. Two in each color: Observatory (red), Aquarium (blue), Garden (green), Library (brown). Obtained by playing 3 consecutive same-color Locations or by discarding a matching Key when drawn.
Cards that trigger special effects when drawn. In the base game, the only Dream cards are the 10 Nightmare cards. Expansion modules add other types of Dream cards.

One of 10 Dream cards in the base game. When drawn, forces you to choose one of four penalties: discard a Key, return a Door to Limbo, reveal and discard top 5 deck cards, or discard your entire hand.
One of three symbols on Location cards, depicted as a sun icon. The most common symbol across all four colors. Cannot be played after another Sun card in the Labyrinth row.
One of three symbols on Location cards, depicted as a crescent moon icon. Less common than Sun symbols. Cannot be played after another Moon card in the Labyrinth row.

One of three symbols on Location cards, depicted as a key icon. The rarest symbol. Key cards have special powers: discarding one triggers a Prophecy, and holding one can claim a drawn Door of matching color.
One of the four labyrinth colors. Has 16 Location cards (the most of any color) and 2 Door cards. Red has 3 Key cards, making it relatively well-supplied for both sequences and Key-based claiming.
One of the four labyrinth colors. Has 15 Location cards and 2 Door cards. Blue has 3 Key cards. Second most common color after red.
One of the four labyrinth colors. Has 14 Location cards and 2 Door cards. Green has only 2 Key cards, making Key management in this color more critical.
One of the four labyrinth colors. Has 13 Location cards (the fewest) and 2 Door cards. Brown has only 2 Key cards. The most difficult color to complete due to card scarcity.
The fundamental restriction on playing cards: the Location card you play into the Labyrinth row cannot share a symbol with the last card in the row. Only the symbol matters, not the color.

Playing three consecutive Location cards of the same color in the Labyrinth row. This triggers Door discovery: search the deck for a Door of that color and place it in your area. A fourth same-color card starts a new series.
Triggered when you discard a Key card during Phase 1. Look at the top 5 cards of the deck, discard one of them, and return the remaining 4 to the top of the deck in any order you choose.
The first phase of each turn. You must either play a Location card from your hand into the Labyrinth row (obeying the golden rule) or discard a card from your hand to the discard pile.

The second phase of each turn. Draw cards from the deck until you have 5 cards in hand. Drawn Location cards go to hand. Drawn Door cards may be claimed with a Key or go to Limbo. Drawn Nightmare cards must be resolved immediately.
The third phase of each turn. If any cards are in the Limbo pile, shuffle them back into the deck. If no cards entered Limbo this turn, skip this phase.

When you draw a Door card during Phase 2, you may discard a Key card of the same color from your hand to immediately claim the Door. The Door goes to your acquired Doors area. The Key goes to the discard pile.

When you play the third consecutive Location card of the same color in your Labyrinth row, search the deck for a Door of that color and place it in your acquired Doors area. Shuffle the deck afterward.

entries.term: Nightmare Penalty I: Discard a Key
entries.definition: Discard one Key card from your hand. If you have no Key in hand, this option is unavailable. The Key goes to the discard pile.

entries.term: Nightmare Penalty II: Return a Door
entries.definition: Place one of your acquired Door cards into the Limbo pile. It will be shuffled back into the deck at end of turn. If you have no acquired Doors, this option is unavailable.
Reveal the top 5 cards of the deck. Discard all Location cards among them. Place any Door or Dream cards into the Limbo pile. This thins the deck but keeps your hand and Doors intact.

Discard your entire hand. Draw a new hand of 5 Location cards using the setup method: set aside any Doors or Dreams to Limbo, draw replacements until you have 5 Locations. Drawn Dreams are not resolved.
In a two-player game, the 3 cards each player holds privately. Combined with the 2 Shared resources, they form your effective 5-card hand.
In a two-player game, the 2 face-up cards on the table that both players can use. When discarding, the active player may swap a Personal card with a Shared card.
Solo: all 8 Door cards on the table simultaneously. Two-player: each player has 4 Doors (one of each color) at the same time. The game ends immediately upon victory.
You must draw a card to fill your hand but the deck is empty. The dream has ended and you are lost in the labyrinth. In a two-player game, defeat occurs if either player cannot draw.
Where cards go when discarded from hand, discarded from Prophecy, or discarded via Nightmare penalties. Cards in the discard pile are out of the game permanently and do not return to the deck.

The face-down draw pile representing the labyrinth. Contains Location cards, Door cards, and Nightmare cards. Cards are drawn from the top during Phase 2. When the deck is empty and a draw is required, the game is lost.

If you play a fourth consecutive Location card of the same color in the Labyrinth row, it counts as the first card of a new series, not a continuation of the previous three. The Door discovery from the first three still applies.
Icons in the lower right corner of cards from expansion modules. These cards should be removed when playing the base game. Expansions are stand-alone and compatible with one another.
